title: Evolution of Gaussian mixed states under the Markovian master equation for a driven quantum oscillator
abstract: We study a generic quantum Markovian master equation for a linearly displaced or driven harmonic oscillator. It was known that the displacement dynamics of Gaussian mixed states depends on the unitary part of the Liouvillian, the decay rate of the system but not on the bath temperature. Here we further show that the fast-rotating modes do not affect the system's displacement dynamics under linear driving forces. Analytical solutions of the quantum master equation are obtained for displaced Gaussian mixed states. Because the non-driven and driven Liouvillians are related by a unitary displacement operator, they are expected to share the same exceptional points structure. At the exceptional points, the displacement of critically damped oscillator displays a characteristics polynomial-in-time prefactor multiplied by an exponential decay. We discuss how external time-dependent forces affect the displacement dynamics using impulsive force and harmonic force as examples. The results obtained for constant driving remain valid in the presence of time-dependent driving.
summary: This paper provides analytical solutions for the evolution of Gaussian states in a driven, dissipative quantum harmonic oscillator. It proves that the drift of the state is independent of bath temperature and fast-rotating modes, and characterizes the system's behavior at exceptional points and under various driving protocols.
main_problem: The paper investigates the time evolution of Gaussian mixed states for a linearly driven quantum harmonic oscillator under a generic Markovian master equation, specifically focusing on how different Liouvillian components affect displacement dynamics.
main_result: The study demonstrates that the displacement dynamics (first moments) are independent of bath temperature and fast-rotating modes, and that the exceptional point structure of the driven system remains identical to the non-driven system.
method: The author uses an analytical approach involving superoperator algebra, displacement superoperators, and biorthogonal eigenbasis solutions to solve the master equation and analyze phase-space trajectories.
model_or_system: A single-mode quantum harmonic oscillator subject to a Markovian master equation of the GKSL type, including unitary evolution, dissipation, and linear driving (constant, impulsive, or harmonic).
key_observables: First moments (average position and momentum) and second moments (variances and squeezing parameters).
important_parameters: Natural frequency (omega_0), decay rate (gamma), bath temperature, driving strength, and the presence of exceptional points (critical damping).
main_assumptions: The system is assumed to be within the Markovian approximation and the states are restricted to the Gaussian regime.
figures_summary: Figure 1(a) shows phase space trajectories under constant driving across different damping regimes; Figure 1(b) illustrates the effect of impulsive driving (kicks) on the oscillator's position.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the master equation and the driven system, moves to deriving analytical solutions for Gaussian states, explores the relationship between driven and non-driven Liouvillians (including exceptional points), and concludes with the analysis of specific time-dependent driving cases (impulsive and harmonic).
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to researchers in open quantum systems and quantum optics as it provides exact analytical solutions for driven-dissipative dynamics and clarifies the role of environmental noise parameters in the evolution of quantum trajectories.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Refer to the main text for the parameters used in the figures. In both figures, gray and black curves denote non-driven and driven oscillator, respectively. Solid curves, dashed curves, and dotted curves label underdamped, critically-damped (at the exceptional point), and overdamped oscillator, respectively. Empty circles indicate initial positions of oscillator. (a) Constant driving. (b) Impulsive driving. When an impulse is introduced, the oscillator is kicked to a new position abruptly. Real impulse with A = 0.5, B = 0 kicks (thin vertical dashed lines) the oscillator vertically at t = 0.5, imaginary impulse with B = 0.5, A = 0 kicks (thin horizontal dashed line) the oscillator...

Figure 2: Harmonic driving. Refer to the main text for the parameters used in the figures. In both figures, gray and black curves denote non- driven and driven oscillator, respectively. Empty circles at (1, 1) denote the initial positions of oscillator. (a) Solid and long-dashed curves label underdamped and critically-damped oscillator (at the exceptional point), respectively. (b) Short-dashed curve labels overdamped oscillator. The oscillator takes a longer time to reach the steady state in the overdamped region.
